question.text: Mr. Sharma, a retired postal officer with 15 years of service, has obtained his Series V-B certification. A client asks him to help invest in a mid-cap fund. What should Mr. Sharma do?

answer.text: Politely decline and explain that mid-cap funds are outside his permitted product range
Go ahead and sell the mid-cap fund since he has a valid certification
Sell it but inform the AMC later
Sell it as a one-time exception

explanation.text: **The Logic:** As a new cadre distributor with Series V-B certification, Mr. Sharma can only sell five specific product categories: Liquid/Money Market, Index, Diversified Equity, Retirement Benefit, and FMPs. A mid-cap fund is a specific category fund and is NOT in his permitted list. He must decline and can refer the client to a regular distributor with V-A certification. **The Trap:** Having a valid certification does not mean you can sell everything. The V-B certification has a limited product scope — unlike the broader V-A certification. **Cross-Exam Context:** This is also tested in Series V-A (MF Distributors) under distributor responsibilities. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Never sell products outside your authorized list — it can lead to regulatory penalties and loss of your ARN. When in doubt, refer the client to a V-A certified distributor.
